script: Ionic bonds form when one atom transfers an electron to another. This creates positively and negatively charged ions that attract each other, holding the atoms together.
Common ionic compounds include sodium chloride, or table salt, and calcium carbonate, found in chalk. These consist of positively and negatively charged ions held together by ionic bonds.
Covalent bonds form when two atoms share electrons. Each atom contributes one electron, and together they create a shared pair that holds the atoms tightly.
Examples of covalent molecules are hydrogen gas, oxygen gas, and water. In each, atoms share electrons to form strong covalent bonds.
Organic compounds contain carbon and hydrogen atoms connected by covalent bonds. Examples include methane, glucose, and larger molecules with many carbon and hydrogen atoms.
It's important to know that 'organic bond' is not a new type of bond. It simply refers to covalent bonds in compounds containing carbon and hydrogen.
